ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a tamarind juice entry; this record is a sweetened diluted tamarind juice drink rather than pure juice. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tamarind juice drink — mapped to fruit juice drinks category under FoodEx2 level 3 hierarchy; sweetened diluted tamarind beverage.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for sweetened tamarind juice drink; A0FKP (fruit juice drinks) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tamarind juice drink as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one glass of approximately 250 ml, providing around 115 kcal and 28.55 g of carbohydrates including 24.28 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Tamarind Juice Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tamarind juice drink (cr_id D03070035). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fruit Juices and Sweetened Beverages — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for tamarind juice drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy with sweetened fruit juice drinks (GI range 55–70 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar sugar-containing beverages). Glycemic load calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (28.55 g per 250 ml serving / 100) = 18.5575, rounded to 18.6. Carbohydrate per serving = 11.42 g/100g × 2.5 = 28.55 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.71 g sugar/100 g and moderate sodium (54 mg/100 g); SSBs independently raise blood pressure via insulin-driven sodium retention.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened tamarind drink; substitute with unsweetened tamarind water, as the 9.71 g/100 g sugar content triggers the SSB hypertension gate and independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines regardless of the moderate sodium level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.71 g sugar/100 g, zero dietary fibre, and medium GI (65); liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial blood glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely, as it is a sugar-sweetened drink with 9.71 g sugar/100 g and no dietary fibre to attenuate glucose absorption; even small servings cause rapid blood glucose elevation and it qualifies as an SSB under diabetes dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (9.71 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage with zero fibre; regular SSB consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides, dyslipidaemia, and increased CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened tamarind drink, as habitual SSB consumption raises triglycerides and contributes to dyslipidaemia; substitute with unsweetened tamarind water to obtain any tamarind polyphenol benefit without the added sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.71 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; no meaningful anti-inflammatory factors present in this diluted sweetened form.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sugar-sweetened tamarind drink, as the added sugar promotes inflammatory mediators and the diluted formulation provides no meaningful polyphenol or anti-inflammatory benefit; unsweetened tamarind water is the preferred substitute.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tamarind Juice Drink is a processed sweetened beverage made from tamarind juice concentrate diluted with water and sweetened with sugar or other sweeteners. It is commonly consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a refreshing drink, particularly as a snack beverage. The drink has a characteristic sweet-sour flavour profile derived from tamarind's natural tartaric acid content, balanced by added sugar. Per 100 g, it provides 46 kcal, 11.42 g carbohydrates (of which 9.71 g is sugar), 0.28 g protein, 0 g fat, 0 g dietary fibre, and 54 mg sodium. The high sugar content relative to its caloric density classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, this drink is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid glucose absorption from liquid sugar with no fibre buffering. It is also unsuitable for hypertension management as SSBs independently raise blood pressure via insulin-driven sodium retention. For cardiovascular disease, regular SSB consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides and dyslipidaemia. The beverage is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is best avoided by those managing metabolic conditions; unsweetened tamarind water is the preferred clinical substitute.

Is tamarind drink good for blood pressure?

Is tamarind juice drink suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03070035) with confidence 0.95. Exact match to the named food item. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for similar sweetened fruit juice drinks, documented accordingly.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; this is a water-based sweetened beverage containing no fat by formulation. Saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; tamarind juice in diluted drink form contains negligible fibre. Calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profile for this tamarind juice drink not available in HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened fruit juice drinks of similar sugar composition; no direct GI measurement for tamarind juice drink was found. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 250 ml serving (250 g): GL = 65 × (11.42 × 2.5) / 100 = 65 × 28.55 / 100 = 18.6. Input description contained a typographical error ('sweetenerss') which has been corrected to 'sweeteners' in processing.
